nodes.label: Formulation Layer S — Stochastic Dynamics and Theorems 4-5
nodes.summary: Layer S extends Layer D's deterministic ODE system (D1-D3) into a coupled SDE system (S1-S3) augmented with four separable device-noise components (thermal, 1/f flicker, device-to-device variability, perturbation-clock jitter). Two theorems are proved on this SDE: Theorem 4 (almost-sure convergence of slow-weight dynamics to a local minimum of the fitness landscape under Robbins-Monro schedule and two-timescale averaging) and Theorem 5 (explicit steady-state weight-error bound as an additive function of device-parameter-indexed noise variances divided by effective learning rate and λ_min(H)). A corollary asserts that the resulting three-tier memory device performs continual learning under data-distribution shift with arbitrary task-interruption tolerance. Section 7 claims formulation completeness (T1-T5 + corollary = existence, realizability, completeness, convergence, noise, archive).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Thermal (kT/C) white noise — σ_th² = k_B T / C_node
nodes.summary: White Gaussian kT/C noise on every capacitive node, fully uncorrelated across cells and time. For charge-trap-site τ_W capacitors at pF scale, thermal voltage noise is ~μV-mV. Appears as σ_x dB^x, σ_δ dB^δ, σ_W dB^W Brownian-motion terms in S1/S2/S3. In Theorem 5 contributes σ_th² = σ_W² + σ_x²·(gain_fwd)² to the bound.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Flicker (1/f) noise — S(ω) = K_f / |ω|^α, α≈1
nodes.summary: Time-correlated 1/f^α noise present on every MOS transistor, dominant in the subthreshold regime that N_β uses for the Gaussian score primitive. Not reducible to Brownian motion; properly modeled by fractional Brownian motion or a 1/f-noise SDE. Enters S1 as (1/f)·dF^x(t). In Theorem 5 contributes σ_flicker²·τ_f, where τ_f ≈ τ_W is the spectral weight of 1/f at the slow-weight timescale. Long-memory behavior at timescales > τ_W is an open problem (§8.1) that could break the OU stationary assumption.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Device-to-device variability — static multiplicative+additive mismatch
nodes.summary: Static but random per-cell offsets: ΔV_T, tail-current mismatch in Gilbert pairs, charge-trap-depth variations. Constant in time post-fabrication but random across the array. Modeled as W_ij^eff = W_ij·(1+ε_ij^mul) + ε_ij^add with ε^mul, ε^add drawn once from fabrication distributions. In Theorem 5 contributes σ_variability² = Var(ε^mul)·‖W*‖² + Var(ε^add).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Perturbation-clock jitter — Poisson event-time noise on ξ
nodes.summary: The counter-RNG produces quasi-deterministic event times (same per seed), but any physical oscillator adds jitter Δt_k ~ 𝒩(0, σ_jitter²). For EGGROLL analysis this appears as a variance in event-count per epoch. In Theorem 5 contributes σ_jitter² = σ_t²·‖∂S/∂t‖² — time-jitter noise propagated through the derivative of the score.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: SDE (S1) — activation dynamics with thermal + flicker noise
nodes.summary: dx(t) = (1/τ_x)·[−x + N(W E[u] + δW E[u])] dt + σ_x dB^x(t) + (1/f)·dF^x(t). Noise-augmented form of ODE (D1): fast activation x tracks the nonlinear combination of weights and input with exponential relaxation τ_x, plus white thermal Brownian motion and 1/f fractional contribution. Independent of S2/S3 Brownian motions.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: SDE (S2) — rank-1 perturbation δW with marked Poisson drive
nodes.summary: dδW(t) = (1/τ_δ)·[−δW] dt + Σ_k a_k b_k^⊤·dN_k(t) + σ_δ dB^δ(t). Perturbation state decays with short τ_δ (leaky integrator) but is periodically kicked by a marked Poisson process N_k with rank-1 marks (a_k, b_k), plus floating-bulk thermal Brownian. In the two-timescale limit τ_W ≫ τ_δ, δW reaches its stationary distribution under the perturbation arrivals before W moves.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: SDE (S3) — slow weight W consolidation with program-write jumps
nodes.summary: dW(t) = (1/τ_W)·[−W + η·S(x, y*)·δW] dt + σ_W dB^W(t) + σ_W^prog·sgn(S δW) dN_prog(t). Slow W evolves on τ_W ≫ τ_δ under fitness-gated consolidation from δW, charge-trap thermal Brownian, and a second programming-pulse Poisson process adding write-noise jumps. Box-constrained to 𝒲 via reflection at saturation boundaries.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ES score-function identity: averaged S3 drift = ∇_W log E[S]
nodes.summary: E_{(a_k,b_k)}[η·S(x,y*)·δW] = η·σ²·∇_W log E_{(a,b)}[S(x(W+ab^⊤), y*)] − η·σ²·∇_W log Z. The Williams-1992 / Wierstra-2014 score-function identity: the averaged rank-1 perturbation update in (S3), taken over the Gaussian perturbation distribution 𝒩(0,σ²), equals a scaled gradient of log-expected-score. ∇log Z vanishes for normalized scores. Conclusion: in the averaged drift dW_avg/dt ≈ η·σ²·∇_W E[S] + noise — EGGROLL's rank-1 perturbation update IS a score-function gradient estimator.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Two-timescale averaging condition τ_W ≫ τ_δ
nodes.summary: Required condition for the ES-score-estimator identity and Theorem 4 Step 1: the slow weight W sees an average over many perturbation marks because its timescale τ_W greatly exceeds the perturbation timescale τ_δ. Formally the condition that Borkar-2008 two-timescale stochastic approximation applies: fast δW reaches stationary distribution on the slow W's timescale. This formalizes Pair 6 (identity η·τ_W/τ_δ).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 4 — Learning convergence of W(t) to a local minimum W* a.s.
nodes.summary: Let u(t) be ergodic with stationary p_data, y*(u) measurable, L(W) := E_u[−S(x(W;u), y*(u))] have isolated local minima with non-degenerate Hessian, η_t = η_0/(1+t/T_0) be Robbins-Monro, and σ_x, σ_δ, σ_W < ∞. Then as t→∞, W(t) converges almost surely to a local minimum W* of L, with liminf ‖W(t)−W*‖ = 0 in any local basin. Does NOT claim global convergence (like any first-order method, ES gradient flow only reaches local minima). The physical τ_W realizes the η_t schedule: larger τ_W = smaller effective learning rate.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 1 — Borkar two-timescale reduction to effective slow SDE
nodes.summary: Borkar-2008 two-timescale stochastic approximation: since τ_W ≫ τ_δ, the fast δW reaches stationary distribution on the timescale of slow W. Averaging (S3) over the fast process replaces δW(t) by its sample mean, yielding the effective slow SDE dW(t) = η(t)·∇_W L(W(t)) dt + σ_avg·dB(t), where σ_avg² aggregates all noise sources (thermal, flicker, write noise) through the averaging operation.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 2 — Lyapunov V(W)=L(W)−L(W*) with Itô's formula
nodes.summary: Take V(W) = L(W) − L(W*) as the candidate Lyapunov function in a local basin around W*. Itô's formula gives dV(W) = −η(t)‖∇L(W)‖² dt + η(t)·(1/2)·tr(σ² H_V(W)) dt + (stochastic part), where H_V is the Hessian. The deterministic drift is strictly negative away from W*; the diffusion term is controlled by σ².
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 3 — Robbins-Monro convergence via Kushner-Yin
nodes.summary: With η(t) Robbins-Monro (∫η dt = ∞, ∫η² dt < ∞), the stochastic-integral martingale term vanishes almost surely. The expected drift E[dV] = −η(t)·(‖∇L‖² − σ²·Tr(H_V)/2) dt is strictly negative near W* for small enough noise (where ‖∇L‖ bounded below by linearity). Classical stochastic approximation (Kushner-Yin 2003) gives V(W(t)) → 0 a.s., hence W(t) → W*.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 4 — Box-constraint feasibility via reflecting boundary
nodes.summary: The W ∈ 𝒲 box constraint (from charge-trap physical saturation, Layer C Tier-2 state) is enforced as a reflecting barrier when the SDE trajectory hits the boundary — standard technique in constrained SDE analysis. Within the interior of 𝒲, Steps 1-3 apply. This closes the proof.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 5 — Noise tolerance: E[‖W(∞)−W*‖²] ≤ C_H·Σσ²/η
nodes.summary: In the stationary regime of (S1)-(S3) near a local minimum W* with Hessian H = ∇²L(W*) ≻ 0, the expected squared deviation satisfies E[‖W(∞)−W*‖²] ≤ C_H·(σ_th² + σ_flicker²·τ_f + σ_variability² + σ_jitter²)/η, with C_H = 1/λ_min(H). Additionally bias(E[W(∞)]) ≤ C_H·σ_ξ/r — the rank-r structure gives the O(r^{-1}) bias-reduction matching EGGROLL's rank convergence theorem. Answers 'what's the worst-case weight-error budget given device thermal X, 1/f Y, variability Z?' — budget set by λ_min(H): well-conditioned losses tolerate more noise, ill-conditioned require tighter control.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 1 — Linearize SDE near W* to Ornstein-Uhlenbeck
nodes.summary: Near a local minimum with full-rank Hessian H, the SDE linearizes to dW(t) = −η·H·(W−W*) dt + σ_eff·dB(t) — an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process with mean W* and covariance determined by H and σ_eff.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 2 — OU stationary covariance via Lyapunov equation
nodes.summary: The stationary covariance Σ = E[(W−W*)(W−W*)^⊤] solves the Lyapunov equation η·H·Σ + η·Σ·H = σ_eff²·I, giving Σ = (σ_eff²/2η)·H^{-1}. Taking trace: E[‖W−W*‖²] = Tr(Σ) = (σ_eff²/2η)·Tr(H^{-1}) ≤ (σ_eff²/2η)·(d/λ_min(H)), where d = nm. Rearranging gives the C_H = 1/λ_min(H) form in Theorem 5.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 3 — Finite-rank-estimator bias ∝ 1/r
nodes.summary: The ES estimator is unbiased in expectation but has finite variance scaling as 1/r for rank-r (standard covariance-rank identity for ES, rank-r EGGROLL paper). Adding this bias to the OU-induced variance gives the full bound bias(E[W(∞)]) ≤ C_H·σ_ξ/r. This formally derives Pair 3 (cell bit-depth → usable rank → noise-floor): low-bit cells cap achievable rank, cap the bias reduction, set a noise floor below which further noise reduction doesn't help.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 4 — Physical noise-source propagation into the bound
nodes.summary: Each σ² term arises from specific physical noise: (i) Thermal σ_th² = k_B T/C with C the smallest capacitor in the forward path; (ii) 1/f with τ_f the spectral weight at frequencies ~τ_W^{-1}; (iii) Variability σ_variability² = Var(ε^mul)·‖W*‖² + Var(ε^add); (iv) Jitter σ_jitter² = σ_t²·‖∂S/∂t‖² through the score derivative. All four contribute additively (independent noises). The bound is thus expressible entirely in device-physics parameters.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Corollary — Continual learning via three-tier memory under distribution shift
nodes.summary: With Tier-3 snapshots triggered by learning-saturation (three_tier_memory_trigger_learning_saturation), archive W̄(t_k) = W(t_k) = W* + ε(t_k) with E[‖ε(t_k)‖²] ≤ Theorem-5 bound — a bias-plus-noise snapshot of the current local minimum. After restore, the device resumes from this bias; if distribution unchanged, restoration is noise-equivalent to one Theorem-5 sample; if shifted, adapts to the new minimum at rate 1/τ_W starting from stored W*. The semi-passive adaptive memory device can perform continual learning under data-distribution shift, with Tier-3 retention giving arbitrarily long task-interruption tolerance (power cycling) while Tier-2 dynamics handle online adaptation.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Prediction 1 — Loss curves should match EGGROLL O(r^{-1}) convergence
nodes.summary: Stage-2 prediction from Theorem 4 + rank analysis: empirical loss curves of the device-in-the-loop simulation should match EGGROLL's O(r^{-1}) rank-convergence scaling. Deviation indicates problems with either the device model (Layer C) or the dynamics (Layer D/S).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Prediction 2 — Steady-state weight MSE equals T5 bound with device σ's
nodes.summary: Stage-2 prediction from Theorem 5: steady-state weight MSE in simulation should equal the Theorem 5 bound with substituted device σ's (σ_th, σ_flicker·√τ_f, σ_variability, σ_jitter). This is the falsifiable core test: if measured MSE exceeds the bound, either the noise model or Theorem 5 is wrong.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Prediction 3 — Distribution-shift recovery on timescale τ_W
nodes.summary: Stage-2 prediction from §6 corollary: after a data-distribution shift, the device adapts to the new local minimum with an exponential approach timescale equal to τ_W (the slow-weight time constant). Deviation indicates Layer C/D/S revision needed.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open problem 1 — 1/f long-memory effects beyond τ_W
nodes.summary: Theorem 5 assumed 1/f contribution integrable over τ_W. If 1/f dominates at timescales > τ_W, the OU stationary assumption (T5 Step 2) fails — in the long run W may exhibit non-stationary drift that Theorem 5 doesn't capture. Expected open until experimental Stage-3-5 data.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open problem 2 — Non-Gaussian perturbation distributions bias gradient estimator
nodes.summary: Theorem 4 relied on Gaussian perturbation for the score-function identity. If the counter-RNG produces sub-Gaussian or heavy-tailed distributions, the gradient estimator is biased. This is a hardware design constraint on the RNG block — RNG must produce approximately Gaussian marks.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open problem 3 — Correlated noise across cells (supply, substrate, bias)
nodes.summary: Power-supply noise, substrate coupling, shared bias lines introduce correlations between σ_W components across cells that Theorem 5 modeled as independent. Correcting gives a d term instead of Tr(H^{-1}) in the bound — possibly much larger for large arrays. Corresponds to Stage-3 cross-tile correlation problem from earlier PPCA note.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Formulation-complete check: T1+T2+T3+T4+T5+corollary
nodes.summary: Section 7 claim: after Layer S Theorems 4 and 5, six check-marks assert the mathematical formulation is complete — well-posedness (T1), operator realizability (T2), signal-processing completeness as switched Volterra operator (T3), learning convergence a.s. (T4), noise tolerance bound (T5), three-tier memory continual-learning corollary (§6). Every question (existence, realizability, completeness, convergence, noise, archive) is answered, all answers expressed in device-physics parameters. Three concrete predictions for Stage-2 simulation listed; three open problems in Section 8.
nodes.author: ws
